PARAGRAPH 1
Developing a robust Python function to filter historical dates by century requires careful attention to the mathematical definition of a century and efficient string processing techniques. Many researchers mistakenly assume that the 19th century includes the year 1900, but historically, the 19th century spans from January 1st, 1801, to December 31st, 1900. This specific constraint means the first year of any given century is calculated as (century minus one) multiplied by one hundred, plus one. For example, the 19th century starts in 1801. The last year is simply the century multiplied by one hundred. Therefore, for the 19th century, the range is 1801 through 1900. To handle a large dataset efficiently, we should avoid converting every single date string into a full date object using the datetime library, as this can be computationally expensive when dealing with millions of entries. Instead, we can leverage the fact that the input strings follow a strict "YYYY-MM-DD" format. By slicing the first four characters of each string, we obtain the year component directly. We can then convert this substring into an integer and compare it against our calculated start and end years. This approach minimizes overhead and maximizes speed. The function will accept a list of date strings and an integer representing the target century. It will iterate through the provided list, extracting the year from each entry. If the year falls within the inclusive range defined by the century logic, the original date string is added to a new result list. This method preserves the original formatting of the dates, ensuring no data is lost or altered during the filtering process. Error handling is also crucial; if a date string does not follow the expected format or contains non-numeric characters in the year section, the function should gracefully skip that entry or raise a clear exception depending on the researcher's preference. For maximum efficiency in very large datasets, list comprehensions in Python are often faster than traditional for-loops because they are optimized at the interpreter level. We can define the start year as (century - 1) * 100 + 1 and the end year as century * 100.
